description:
A cloud-architect is cleaning up a micro-services mono-repo before migrating it to a managed Kubernetes
  cluster.  
  Your goal is to produce a **portable “config bundle”** that contains every  
  “service-level” configuration file (those ending in “.yaml” or “.env”) that lives
  anywhere under the directory
  
      /home/user/projects/microservices
  
  The architect wants to keep the original tree intact while also creating a
  self-contained copy of those configuration files for offline review.

  1.  Create the target directory
  
          /home/user/projects/config_backup
  
      if it does not already exist.

  2.  Use a combination of the commands **find** and **xargs** (no interactive
      prompting) to **copy**, not move, every file that matches the glob patterns  
      “*.yaml” and “*.env” found below
  
          /home/user/projects/microservices
  
      into the new directory.  
      •  Preserve the *relative* directory layout of each file inside the
         destination directory so that, for example,
  
             /home/user/projects/microservices/serviceA/deploy.yaml
  
         becomes
  
             /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceA/deploy.yaml
  
      •  Non-matching files (e.g., “readme.txt”, source code, etc.) must **not**
         appear in the backup directory.

  3.  Produce a manifest file named
  
          /home/user/projects/config_backup/backup_log.txt
  
      that is a plain-text list of the **absolute paths inside the backup
      directory** for every copied file.

      backup_log.txt must follow this exact format so automated tests can
      validate it:

      •  One path per line.  
      •  Each path must be the full, absolute path that points **to the copy
         inside /home/user/projects/config_backup/**.  
      •  Lines must be in ordinary *lexicographical* (ASCII) ascending order.  
      •  No blank lines.  
      •  A trailing newline (`\n`) after the very last line is required.

      Example of correct formatting (paths will differ in your case):

          /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceA/.env
          /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceA/deploy.yaml
          <…more lines…>

  4.  You should not delete or alter the originals under
      /home/user/projects/microservices.

  Verification checklist (what the automated grader will look for):

      •  Directory “/home/user/projects/config_backup” exists.  
      •  Exactly six files (three “.yaml”, three “.env”) are present inside that
         directory tree, in the same relative locations they had under
         “microservices”.  
      •  The originals still exist in their original place.  
      •  backup_log.txt exists, is readable, and its contents exactly match the
         specification above.

  You are free to create helper sub-directories or temporary files if you need
  them, but they may not be required for passing the tests.

  IMPORTANT RESTRICTIONS:
      •  Do not move or rename any file inside /home/user/projects/microservices.  
      •  Do not use tools that require interactive confirmation.  
      •  Root privileges are not available.

  Hints (do not output the commands directly; derive them yourself):
      •  “find … -type f \( -name '*.yaml' -o -name '*.env' \) …”
      •  “cp --parents” is one handy way to preserve directory structure.
      •  “printf '%s\n' … | sort …” is a simple, POSIX-compliant way to create a
         sorted manifest.

  Once finished, the architect can tar up “/home/user/projects/config_backup”
  and ship the bundle to the compliance team.
truth:
<!--
    Initial repository layout that is guaranteed to be present when the task starts.
    All paths are absolute and will exist with 0644 permissions (directories 0755).

    /home/user/projects/microservices/
        serviceA/
            deploy.yaml     (content: "version: 'A'\n")
            .env            (content: "SERVICE=serviceA\nDB=mysql\n")
            readme.txt      (content: "Service A documentation\n")
        serviceB/
            app.yaml        (content: "service: B\nreplicas: 3\n")
            .env            (content: "SERVICE=serviceB\nDB=postgres\n")
        serviceC/
            infra.yaml      (content: "infra: true\nregion: us-east-1\n")
            .env            (content: "SERVICE=serviceC\nDB=mongodb\n")

    No directory /home/user/projects/config_backup/ exists at task start.

    After correct completion the grader will assert:

    1. The directory /home/user/projects/config_backup exists.
    2. The following paths, and only these paths, exist as regular files:

        /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceA/deploy.yaml
        /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceA/.env
        /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceB/app.yaml
        /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceB/.env
        /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceC/infra.yaml
        /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceC/.env

       Their byte-for-byte contents must match the originals.

    3. The file /home/user/projects/config_backup/backup_log.txt exists and its
       exact byte content equals the six absolute paths above, sorted
       lexicographically, each terminated by a single “\n”.

       For reference, the correct final content (including the final newline) is:

           /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceA/.env\n
           /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceA/deploy.yaml\n
           /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceB/.env\n
           /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceB/app.yaml\n
           /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceC/.env\n
           /home/user/projects/config_backup/serviceC/infra.yaml\n

    4. The original six configuration files still exist at their original paths
       under /home/user/projects/microservices/ with unchanged contents.

    5. No additional “.yaml” or “.env” files are present inside
       /home/user/projects/config_backup/.

    The grader fixes its expectations exactly on the above conditions.
  -->
name: task_000000_3ec7636b
